articles.label: Government
articles.headline: Escambia County faces $12 million shortfall as tourism tax revenue falls short of projections
articles.dek: Commission meets Thursday to consider cuts to road maintenance and parks funding ahead of hurricane season
brief: Escambia County is projecting a $12 million budget shortfall driven by lower-than-expected tourist development tax collections
County administrators have proposed cutting $4.2 million from road resurfacing and $3.1 million from parks maintenance
The Board of County Commissioners will hold a special budget workshop Thursday to consider options before the June hurricane season
articles.body:
The folder sitting in front of County Administrator Wes Moreno was three inches thick. He had read every page twice. None of the numbers had improved the second time through.

"We built this year's budget on assumptions that didn't hold," Moreno told The Flightline in an interview Monday. "Now we're looking at hard choices with hurricane season eight weeks away."

Escambia County is staring down a $12 million shortfall in its $412 million general fund budget, according to a fiscal analysis released Friday by the county's Office of Management and Budget. The gap stems primarily from tourist development tax collections that came in 18 percent below projections through the first six months of the fiscal year.

The county had budgeted $34.6 million in bed tax revenue. Current projections show collections will land closer to $28.4 million by September 30.

What went wrong

County budget officials point to a combination of factors. A cooler-than-normal spring kept beach visitors away in March and early April. Two major hotels on Pensacola Beach completed renovations later than expected, leaving rooms offline during peak booking season. And gas prices that topped $4.20 a gallon in February appear to have dampened drive-in tourism from Alabama and Georgia.

"We saw the warning signs in January," said Budget Director Patricia Vance. "But you don't want to overreact to one bad month. By March it was clear this wasn't a blip."

Commissioner Jeff Bergosh, whose District 1 includes Pensacola Beach, said the commission bears some responsibility for aggressive revenue estimates.

"We wanted to believe the post-pandemic boom would continue," Bergosh said. "The hospitality industry was telling us bookings looked strong. They were wrong, and we were wrong to take their word for it."

Proposed cuts

Moreno's office has proposed closing the gap through a combination of spending cuts and reserve fund transfers. The most significant reductions would hit infrastructure:

Road resurfacing would lose $4.2 million, reducing this year's planned work from 89 miles to approximately 52 miles. The county's pavement condition index has already fallen from 72 to 68 over the past three years, approaching the threshold where roads require full reconstruction rather than resurfacing.

Parks and recreation would see a $3.1 million reduction. Planned improvements to Brent Athletic Park and Maritime Park would be delayed until fiscal year 2027. Seasonal staffing at county beaches would drop by roughly 40 positions.

The remaining $4.7 million would come from contingency reserves, leaving the county with $8.3 million in emergency funds heading into hurricane season. County policy recommends maintaining reserves equal to at least 60 days of operating expenses, or roughly $11 million.

Thursday's workshop

The Board of County Commissioners will meet in special session Thursday at 1 p.m. to review the proposed cuts. The meeting will be held in the Ernie Lee Magaha Government Building on Palafox Street.

Commissioner Lumon May said he wants to see alternatives before agreeing to infrastructure cuts.

"Every year we push off road work, the bill gets bigger," May said. "I need to understand what we're really saving here versus what we're kicking down the road for the next commission to deal with."

Commissioner Steven Barry suggested the county consider a short-term loan rather than depleting reserves before hurricane season.

"We had $14 million in storm damage from Hurricane Sally that FEMA didn't reimburse for three years," Barry said. "I'm not comfortable going into June with $8 million in the bank."

Public comment will be accepted at Thursday's workshop. Residents can also submit written comments through the county's online portal through Wednesday at 5 p.m.

The commission must adopt any budget amendments by May 15 to comply with state law.

articles.label: Development
articles.headline: Construction delays push Palafox parking garage opening to fall, leaving downtown merchants in limbo
articles.dek: The $28 million project was supposed to ease parking pressure before summer tourist season
brief: The new Palafox Street parking garage will not open until September at the earliest, missing the summer tourist season entirely
Supply chain issues and unexpected soil conditions added four months to the construction timeline
Downtown business owners say the prolonged construction zone has already cost them customers
articles.body:
The orange construction fencing has been up so long on South Palafox that moss is starting to grow on it. Seventeen months now. The fence was supposed to come down in May.

It won't. Not even close.

The City of Pensacola announced Monday that the $28 million Palafox Street parking garage will not open until September 2026 at the earliest—four months behind schedule and well after the summer tourist rush that downtown merchants desperately needed it for.

"We're looking at a mid-September completion date, assuming no further setbacks," said Marcus Webb, the city's director of public works, at a briefing for downtown stakeholders Monday afternoon. "I know that's not what anyone wants to hear."

It is not.

What went wrong

The six-story, 540-space garage broke ground in November 2024. The original timeline called for completion by late May 2026—timed, city officials said repeatedly, to have the structure operational before Memorial Day weekend.

Two problems derailed that plan.

The first emerged in March 2025, when crews discovered the soil beneath the site was less stable than initial surveys indicated. The fix required driving additional foundation piles 40 feet deeper than designed. That added six weeks to the schedule and $1.2 million to the budget.

The second problem proved more stubborn. Structural steel deliveries from the project's primary supplier, a fabricator in Birmingham, fell behind starting last fall. Webb attributed the delays to what he called "ongoing supply chain disruptions" but declined to elaborate on whether the city had explored alternative suppliers.

"We're working with the contractor to identify every possible efficiency," Webb said. "But you can't pour concrete floors without the steel to hold them up."

The contractor, Emerald Coast Builders of Fort Walton Beach, did not respond to requests for comment.

Summer without relief

For downtown business owners, the timing couldn't be worse.

Parking has been the chronic complaint of Palafox Street commerce for years. The garage was pitched as the solution—a centrally located structure that would add 540 spaces to a district where weekend visitors routinely circle blocks looking for a spot.

Instead, the construction site itself has made things harder. The project eliminated 78 surface parking spaces and forced the temporary closure of the small lot behind the Saenger Theatre. Delivery trucks have had to navigate around equipment. Dust coats storefront windows on dry days.

"I've had customers tell me flat out they stopped coming downtown because of the construction," said Elena Marchetti, owner of Gulf Breeze Books on South Palafox. "They'll say 'Let me know when it's done.' Well, now I have to tell them September. Maybe."

Marchetti said her sales are down roughly 15 percent compared to last spring. She can't attribute all of that to the garage project, she said, but the construction zone hasn't helped.

Two blocks north, restaurant owner David Chen said he's been adjusting staff schedules in anticipation of a slower summer. His place, a Vietnamese spot that opened in 2023, depends heavily on foot traffic from tourists and the weekend bar crowd.

"June, July, August—that's when we make our money," Chen said. "You bank it for the slow months. If those months are down, everything gets tight."

City's response

Mayor Grover Robinson's office released a statement Monday expressing "disappointment" with the timeline but confidence in the project's eventual completion.

"The Palafox garage will be a transformative addition to our downtown infrastructure," the statement read. "While we regret the delays, we remain committed to delivering a quality facility that serves our community for decades to come."

The statement did not address whether the city would pursue any financial penalties against the contractor for missing the deadline. Webb said the contract includes liquidated damages provisions but declined to discuss specifics.

City Council member Delaria Wiliams, whose District 6 includes downtown, said she plans to request a detailed briefing on the project's cost overruns and timeline slippage at the next council meeting.

"My constituents deserve a full accounting," Williams said. "Not just 'supply chain issues.' What decisions were made, when, and by whom."

What comes next

Webb said crews are now working six-day weeks to compress the remaining schedule. The structural steel is expected to be fully in place by late May, allowing interior work to proceed through the summer.

The garage will feature electric vehicle charging stations on two levels, a ground-floor retail space the city hopes to lease to a coffee shop or similar business, and a rooftop level with views of Pensacola Bay. Hourly rates have not been finalized but are expected to range from $1.50 to $2.00 per hour, with monthly passes available for downtown workers.

For now, the orange fencing stays up. The construction noise continues. The parking hunt goes on.

Marchetti, the bookstore owner, said she's trying to stay optimistic. The garage will open eventually. The dust will settle. Customers will return.

"But September," she said, shaking her head. "That's a long time to wait for eventually."

articles.label: NAS Pensacola
articles.headline: Blue Angels announce expanded practice schedule ahead of 80th anniversary celebration
articles.dek: Base officials work with city on traffic management as July event expected to draw record crowds
brief: Blue Angels will add four extra practice sessions in June ahead of July 80th anniversary show
City and base officials coordinating traffic plan for expected 200,000+ visitors over celebration weekend
Anniversary event will feature historic aircraft displays and reunions of former demonstration pilots
articles.body:
Six F/A-18 Super Hornets screamed over Pensacola Beach last Tuesday morning, banking hard over the Gulf before pulling into a vertical climb that left white contrails scratched against the blue sky. A couple dozen tourists on the sand stopped mid-stride to watch. They'll have plenty more chances this summer.

The Blue Angels will conduct four additional practice sessions in June beyond their normal Wednesday-Thursday schedule, base officials announced Monday. The expanded practices come as NAS Pensacola prepares for the 80th anniversary celebration of the Navy's flight demonstration squadron, scheduled for July 10-12.

"This is going to be the largest event in the squadron's history," said Capt. Marcus Webb, commanding officer of NAS Pensacola, during a press briefing at the National Naval Aviation Museum. "We want to make sure we're ready."

Base officials expect between 200,000 and 250,000 visitors over the three-day celebration weekend. That would surpass the previous record of approximately 175,000 attendees set during the 75th anniversary event in 2021.

Traffic coordination underway

The crowd projections have city and county officials working overtime on logistics. Pensacola City Council member Jenna Harwood, whose District 6 includes the main corridors leading to the base, said traffic management discussions began in February.

"We learned a lot from the 75th," Harwood said. "The bottleneck on Navy Boulevard was brutal. People sat in traffic for two hours trying to get off base."

The joint traffic plan, still being finalized, will include temporary signal timing changes on Navy Boulevard, Barrancas Avenue and Blue Angel Parkway. Escambia County will provide additional sheriff's deputies for traffic control, and the city is negotiating with ECAT for expanded shuttle service from remote parking locations.

Webb said the base will open five parking areas instead of the usual three, utilizing space near Corry Station and the outlying fields east of the museum. Shuttle buses will run continuously from 7 a.m. to 8 p.m. each day of the event.

Historic aircraft and pilot reunions

The anniversary celebration will feature more than the current team's performance. Museum director Robert Chen said the weekend will include static displays of every aircraft the Blue Angels have flown since the squadron's founding in 1946.

"We're bringing in an F6F Hellcat, which was the original demonstration aircraft," Chen said. "We've got an F-11 Tiger coming from the Smithsonian. The F-4 Phantom will be here."

The museum has tracked down flyable examples of five of the eight aircraft types in Blue Angels history. The A-4 Skyhawk and F-4 Phantom will perform heritage flights alongside the current Super Hornets during Saturday's main show.

More than 60 former Blue Angels pilots have confirmed attendance for a reunion dinner Friday evening at the museum. Three pilots from the 1950s-era teams will be present, including retired Rear Adm. William "Butch" Rollins, 94, who flew with the squadron in 1957 and 1958.

Extra practice schedule

The additional June practices are scheduled for June 2, 9, 16 and 23, all Tuesdays. Standard Wednesday-Thursday practices will continue as normal. The team typically practices Tuesday through Thursday but only opens the final two days to public viewing.

Beginning June 2, all Tuesday practices will also be open to spectators at the museum viewing area.

"The extra sessions give us time to rehearse the anniversary-specific formations," said Cmdr. Alex Dietrich, the Blue Angels' commanding officer and flight leader. "We're adding some elements that reference the squadron's history. I don't want to spoil it, but people who know the old routines will recognize what we're doing."

Dietrich, who took command in September 2025, said the team has been studying archival footage of performances from the 1940s through the 1970s.

The museum viewing area opens at 9 a.m. on practice days. The team typically begins flying around 11:30 a.m., weather permitting. No tickets or reservations are required for practices. The museum and its grounds are free and open to the public.

For the July anniversary event, the base will require advance registration for parking passes beginning June 1. Details will be posted on the NAS Pensacola website and the Blue Angels' official social media accounts.
